questions.question: You install `minima` as a Ruby gem and want to read its `_layouts/default.html`. Where does that file actually live on disk?
choices: In your blog repo's `_layouts/` directory, created by `jekyll new`
Inside the installed gem's directory, which you can locate with `bundle info minima`
Inside `_site/`, generated on the first build
On GitHub only - Jekyll fetches it at build time
questions.explanation: Gem-based theme files are not copied into your repo. They live inside the gem's install path and Jekyll reads them at build time; `bundle info minima` prints that path.

questions.question: Your design brief calls for a single-column blog with system fonts, a small header, and posts in reverse-chronological order. You also want to be able to change a few `<meta>` tags and the link colour. Which starting point matches the work best?
choices: A blank `index.html` from scratch, so you control every byte
Fork `jekyll-theme-chirpy` and remove the features you don't want
Start with `minima` and override only `_includes/head.html` and a Sass variable
Skip a theme entirely and rely on the browser's default stylesheet
questions.explanation: The brief matches what `minima` already does; the customisations needed are a couple of overrides. Forking Chirpy means inheriting a lot of opinions you didn't ask for, and from-scratch is more maintenance than the brief justifies.

questions.question: What is the strongest argument for preferring a gem-based theme over forking a community theme repo?
choices: Gem-based themes are always faster at build time
Forks can't be deployed to GitHub Pages
Gem-based themes let you pull upstream fixes via `bundle update` instead of merging conflicts in your tree
Forks force you to use Ruby, whereas gem-based themes do not
questions.explanation: With a gem-based theme you own only the files you've overridden, so upstream updates arrive cleanly as `bundle update`. A fork pulls every file into your repo, turning future upstream changes into merge conflicts.
